did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning methodologies, specifically a proposed fusion-based deep learning strategy (including DenseNet-161) and conventional machine learning models (Random Forest, SVM, KNN), to detect stages of melanoma using the HAM10000 and ISIC 2018 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a proposed fusion-based deep learning methodology for detecting melanoma stages. It utilizes specific datasets (PH2 and HAM10000), implements pre-processing and feature extraction techniques (GLCM, KNN clustering), and reports new quantitative results (97% accuracy) comparing the proposed model against state-of-the-art techniques, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Detection of stages of melanoma using deep learning
brief_summary: This study addresses the detection and staging of melanoma using a proposed fusion-based deep learning methodology. The researchers utilized the PH2 and HAM10000 (ISIC 2018) datasets, employing pre-processing techniques like median/Gaussian filters and KNN clustering to ensure lesion relevance. The model integrates Haralick GLCM features with deep learning architectures, specifically comparing Random Forest, SVM, and DenseNet-161. Evaluation metrics included accuracy, precision, recall, MSE, and PSNR to assess both image quality and classification performance. The proposed deep learning approach achieved a superior accuracy of 97%, outperforming conventional models and existing state-of-the-art techniques by approximately 11%.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary focus of the study is the detection and staging of melanoma. The PH2 dataset used contained 140 melanoma images. The HAM10000 dataset was also used for training and evaluation. The paper classifies melanoma into four stages: Superficial, Nodular, Lentigo malignant, and Acral Lentiginous.

Used as the negative class or comparator to melanoma. The PH2 dataset included 160 non-melanoma images. The HAM10000 dataset contains various skin lesion types including benign and malignant non-melanoma classes. The model distinguishes between melanoma and non-melanoma/normal lesions.
Mentioned in the introduction as a type of Non-melanoma skin cancer (NMSC) that develops on the surface of the skin, distinct from melanoma. Not used as a specific target class in the experiments.
Mentioned in the introduction as a type of Non-melanoma skin cancer (NMSC). Not used as a specific target class in the experiments.

all_task_types.target: Stages of melanoma (Superficial, Nodular, Lentigo malignant, Acral Lentiginous) and Melanoma vs Non-melanoma
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is to 'detect stages of melanoma' and 'affirm the current stage of the melanoma'. It classifies melanoma into four specific stages (Superficial, Nodular, Lentigo malignant, Acral Lentiginous) and also distinguishes between melanoma and non-melanoma skin lesions. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories using a fusion-based deep learning strategy (DenseNet-161) and conventional classifiers (Random Forest, SVM, KNN). Performance is evaluated using Accuracy, Precision, and Recall, which are standard metrics for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dermoscopy image dataset used for the initial training of the proposed deep neural networks. It contains images of skin lesions acquired from subjects.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (ISIC 2018 Challenge Dataset)
datasets_used.description: A larger public dataset archived by ISIC, containing skin lesion images acquired using MoleMax HD system (1872x1053 resolution) and compressed to 800x600. The study utilized a specific subset of malignant melanoma images from this dataset.

use.other: Used to address the limitations of the PH2 dataset by providing a larger variety of features and more training-validation subsets.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported in the paper.

Proposed DL.accuracy: Not explicitly reported as a final number, but described as 'not satisfactory' due to limited variety of features and subjects.

PH2 Dataset.notes: The PH2 dataset (140 melanoma, 160 non-melanoma images) was used for initial training. The performance was unsatisfactory, prompting the use of the HAM10000 dataset.

HAM10000 (ISIC 2018) Dataset.notes: Results are based on 10-fold cross-validation on 1113 malignant melanoma images. The proposed DL model achieved 96.7% accuracy when using all features (Size + Colour + Texture). The abstract mentions 97% accuracy, while Table 5 specifies 96.7% for DenseNet-161 on the ISIC 2018 dataset. Table 4 shows accuracy variations by feature set for the proposed DL: Size (89.5%), Size + Colour (93.3%), Size + Colour + Texture (96.7%). The proposed model outperformed state-of-the-art techniques by approximately 11%.

ISIC 2017 Challenge Dataset.notes: These results are from related works [19] used for comparison in Table 5, not the primary experimental results of the proposed method in this paper.

architecture_details.feature_fusion: Sum rule fusion of Haralick GLCM features (Size, Colour, Texture) with deep learning layers

training_details.training_schedule: 10-fold cross-validation on HAM10000 dataset (1113 malignant melanoma images)
training_details.validation_strategy: 10-fold cross-validation (111 images for validation in each fold, rest 1002 split into 10 bins for training/validation)

classification_or_output_head.type: Softmax classifier (implied by context of classification and comparison to other works using softmax)
classification_or_output_head.classes: 4 stages of melanoma (Superficial, Nodular, Lentigo malignant, Acral Lentiginous) and Melanoma vs Non-melanoma

preprocessing_techniques: Combination of Median and Gaussian Filter
KNN clustering to ensure lesion relevance (filtering for images with 50% moles having different pattern)
Active contour segmentation for ROI extraction
conclusions: The proposed fusion-based deep learning methodology, which integrates Haralick GLCM features with DenseNet-161, achieved an accuracy of 97% (specifically 96.7% in detailed results) for detecting melanoma stages, outperforming state-of-the-art techniques by approximately 11%.

The proposed deep learning model demonstrated superior performance compared to conventional machine learning models, including Random Forest (85.7% accuracy) and Support Vector Machine (91.2% accuracy).

The PH2 dataset was found to be insufficient for robust training due to its limited number of subjects and variety of features, leading to unsatisfactory performance; consequently, the larger HAM10000 dataset was utilized to address these limitations.

The pre-processing strategy, combining Median and Gaussian filters with local histogram equalization, significantly improved image quality, as evidenced by high Peak Signal to Noise Ratio (PSNR) and low Mean Square Error (MSE) metrics.
The use of KNN clustering prior to experimentation helped ensure lesion relevance by filtering for images with specific mole pattern properties, thereby improving the reliability of the input data.
The study confirms that premature detection of melanoma stages is critical for effective treatment and reducing mortality rates, validating the clinical relevance of automated staging systems.
important_remarks: The study focuses exclusively on malignant melanoma (1113 images from HAM10000) for staging, rather than a multi-class classification of all skin lesion types, limiting its generalizability to broader dermatological screening tasks.
The initial PH2 dataset was abandoned due to 'limited variety of features' and 'limited number of subjects,' indicating a significant dataset bias and lack of robustness in early experimental phases.

The paper claims 97% accuracy in the abstract and text, but Table 4 and Table 5 report the specific accuracy for the proposed DenseNet-161 model as 96.7%, representing a minor inconsistency in reported results.

The comparison with state-of-the-art methods in Table 5 relies on external literature values (e.g., Inception-v4, ResNet-152 from [19]) rather than re-running those models on the same experimental setup, which may introduce unfair comparison biases.

The pre-processing pipeline includes aggressive compression (to 64x64 pixels) and KNN clustering to filter for '50% moles having different pattern,' which may introduce selection bias and reduce the diversity of the training data.

The authors acknowledge that the proposed DenseNet-161 model is 'time consuming' (14.2 seconds execution time vs. 13.8s for CNN in Table 3), raising concerns about real-time clinical deployment efficiency.

No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated solely using 10-fold cross-validation on a single public dataset (HAM10000 subset), limiting evidence of generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
The study does not report standard deviation or confidence intervals for the accuracy metrics, only mean values, which weakens the statistical rigor of the performance claims.
